patches_submitted.subject: [RFC PATCH] mm: bypass swap readahead for zswap

version_history.subject: [RFC PATCH] mm: bypass swap readahead for zswap

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch bypasses swap readahead for zswap, an in-memory compression backend similar to zram. The current implementation of swap readahead is optimized for traditional disk-based swap devices and can cause issues like inflated anon residency, page cache thrashing, and increased fault latency when applied to an in-memory backend. By skipping readahead for zswap, the patch aims to improve performance by reducing unnecessary prefetching of pages that are not needed. The results from testing this bypass show significant improvements in build time, swap-in/out rates, and reduced swap readahead hits.

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that bypassing swap readahead for zswap is an interesting idea but did not provide any specific technical feedback or concerns.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Alexandre

Thanks for the test and patch, very interesting idea.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: The reviewer suggested moving the bypass logic for zswap readahead into a separate, named function within the swap code to improve abstraction and maintainability.
review_comments.raw_body:
This should really be abstracted into a reasonably-named helper that can live in
swap code.

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Nhat Pham agreed with the patch, suggesting an additional improvement by calling xa_empty() to further optimize zswap performance.
review_comments.raw_body:
+1.

Maybe also xa_empty() when we're at it? :)

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: The reviewer questioned the assumption that if a swap entry is in zswap, then all subsequent readahead entries will also be in zswap, pointing out that it's possible for remaining entries to be on disk instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
This assumes that if the swap entry is in zswap, then the remaining
entries (covered by the readahead window) will also be in zswap,
right? While not very likely, it's possible that the remaining entries
not in zswap but on disk, right?

review_comments.author: Barry Song
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Barry Song noted that readahead swap entries in zswap can lead to unnecessary decompression and additional page faults, and suggested a proof-of-concept patch to enable fault-around-like behavior for do_swap_page() when the readahead folios are in zswap.

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH] btrfs: print-tree: print header owner as signed
patches_acked.patch_summary: Patch to print header owner as signed in btrfs print-tree, sparking discussion on complexity and practicality.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary: Suggested a macro or function wrapping a switch statement to print string names of known trees, but acknowledged the patch is an improvement.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Expressed concerns about adding complexity for kernel modules and suggested a signed s64 output as sufficient for most users.
review_comments.raw_body:
\u5728 2026/6/24 13:56, Boris Burkov \u5199\u9053:
> On Tue, Jun 23, 2026 at 08:25:40AM +0930, Qu Wenruo wrote:
>> When dumpping a tree block, btrfs_header::owner is printed as
>> unsigned, which can result in numbers that are hard to read, e.g:
>>
>>   BTRFS info (device loop0): leaf 8908800 gen 16 total ptrs 28 free space 1676 owner 18446744073709551607
>>
>> For the above output, 18446744073709551607 is (s64)-9, the root id of data
>> reloc tree.
>>
>> Despite those pre-defined root ids that are already negative, existing
>> subvolume trees will not have any negative values, as subvolume trees can
>> only utilize the lower 48 bits, so there will be no output change for
>> existing subvolumes, thus no extra confusion.
>>
> 
> What do you think about a macro or function wrapping a switch statement
> that also prints the string name of known trees?

That is possible, but I'm not sure if we need to go this complex for 
kernel modules.

A signed s64 output would be enough for most developers/advanced users 
to debug.
For end users, I do not know if things like 2 or EXTENT_TREE make any 
difference.

> What about some identifying
> info like a volume path for subvol trees? That one might be a stretch.

That's is too dangerous, it will need extra tree search and backref lookup.
Meanwhile for a tree dump inside a kernel, it's already an emergency, 
normally we do not have the extra headroom for such complex workload 
just to make the subvolume id a little easier to read.

Thanks,
Qu

> 
> Anyway, this is already a definite improvement, thanks.
> Reviewed-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>
> 
>> Signed-off-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>
>> ---
>>   fs/btrfs/print-tree.c | 8 ++++----
>>   1 file changed, 4 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)
>>
>> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/print-tree.c b/fs/btrfs/print-tree.c
>> index 87e60a2d4bd8..53e726119ca7 100644
>> --- a/fs/btrfs/print-tree.c
>> +++ b/fs/btrfs/print-tree.c
>> @@ -449,9 +449,9 @@ void btrfs_print_leaf(const struct extent_buffer *l)
>>   	nr = btrfs_header_nritems(l);
>>   
>>   	btrfs_info(fs_info,
>> -		   "leaf %llu gen %llu total ptrs %d free space %d owner %llu",
>> +		   "leaf %llu gen %llu total ptrs %d free space %d owner %lld",
>>   		   btrfs_header_bytenr(l), btrfs_header_generation(l), nr,
>> -		   btrfs_leaf_free_space(l), btrfs_header_owner(l));
>> +		   btrfs_leaf_free_space(l), (s64)btrfs_header_owner(l));
>>   	print_eb_refs_lock(l);
>>   	for (i = 0 ; i < nr ; i++) {
>>   		char key_buf[KEY_TYPE_BUF_SIZE];
>> @@ -600,10 +600,10 @@ void btrfs_print_tree(const struct extent_buffer *c, bool follow)
>>   		return;
>>   	}
>>   	btrfs_info(fs_info,
>> -		   "node %llu level %d gen %llu total ptrs %d free spc %u owner %llu",
>> +		   "node %llu level %d gen %llu total ptrs %d free spc %u owner %lld",
>>   		   btrfs_header_bytenr(c), level, btrfs_header_generation(c),
>>   		   nr, (u32)BTRFS_NODEPTRS_PER_BLOCK(fs_info) - nr,
>> -		   btrfs_header_owner(c));
>> +		   (s64)btrfs_header_owner(c));
>>   	print_eb_refs_lock(c);
>>   	for (i = 0; i < nr; i++) {
>>   		btrfs_node_key_to_cpu(c, &key, i);
>> -- 
>> 2.54.0
>>

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH] btrfs: use correct type for sectorsize/nodesize/blocksize
Re: [PATCH] btrfs: change block group reclaim_mark to bool
patches_acked.patch_summary: Patch to change block group reclaim_mark from int to bool in btrfs.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with minor suggestions for comment polishing. Identified a grammar issue with the word 'Mark' and proposed alternative wording.

review_comments.author: Sun YangKai
review_comments.summary: Responded to Boris' suggestions, proposing an alternative wording for the comment and thanking him for his input.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 2026/6/24 12:19, Boris Burkov wrote:
> On Wed, Jun 24, 2026 at 12:02:27PM +0800, Sun YangKai wrote:
>> The reclaim_mark field in struct btrfs_block_group was a u64 that was
>> incremented when marking block groups for reclaim during sweeping, but
>> the actual counter value was never used - only the zero/non-zero state
>> mattered for determining if a block group needed reclaim.
>>
>> Convert it to a bool to properly reflect its usage and reduce memory
>> footprint by 8 bytes. Update assignments to use true/false instead of
>> increment and zero.
>>
> 
> LGTM, thanks.
> Reviewed-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>
> 
>> Signed-off-by: Sun YangKai <sunk67188@gmail.com>
>> ---
>>   fs/btrfs/block-group.c | 2 +-
>>   fs/btrfs/block-group.h | 7 ++++++-
>>   fs/btrfs/space-info.c  | 2 +-
>>   3 files changed, 8 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)
>>
>> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/block-group.c b/fs/btrfs/block-group.c
>> index 9e937407d12a9..5829c806c4d48 100644
>> --- a/fs/btrfs/block-group.c
>> +++ b/fs/btrfs/block-group.c
>> @@ -3923,7 +3923,7 @@ int btrfs_update_block_group(struct btrfs_trans_handle *trans,
>>   		old_val += num_bytes;
>>   		cache->used = old_val;
>>   		cache->reserved -= num_bytes;
>> -		cache->reclaim_mark = 0;
>> +		cache->reclaim_mark = false;
>>   		space_info->bytes_reserved -= num_bytes;
>>   		space_info->bytes_used += num_bytes;
>>   		space_info->disk_used += num_bytes * factor;
>> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/block-group.h b/fs/btrfs/block-group.h
>> index 790c2d467af57..942befdf75df7 100644
>> --- a/fs/btrfs/block-group.h
>> +++ b/fs/btrfs/block-group.h
>> @@ -263,6 +263,12 @@ struct btrfs_block_group {
>>   
>>   	enum btrfs_block_group_size_class size_class:8;
>>   
>> +	/*
>> +	 * Mark this blockgroup is not used for allocation
>> +	 * between two reclaim sweeps.
> 
> nit: this grammar doesn't parse great to me. Maybe drop "Mark"?
> or change it to "We identified this for reclaim on a previous pass, so
> it stably needs reclaim across passes" or something like that.

I also think this comment should be polished but have no idea. Your nit 
helps a lot. How about this:

Tracks whether this blockgroup has been idle during the previous 
periodic sweep cycle. It is set to false on allocation, and set to true 
by the periodic sweep upon first pass. If found true on a subsequent 
scan, the blockgroup is eligible for reclamation.

>> +	 */
>> +	bool reclaim_mark;
>> +
>>   	/*
>>   	 * Number of extents in this block group used for swap files.
>>   	 * All accesses protected by the spinlock 'lock'.
>> @@ -281,7 +287,6 @@ struct btrfs_block_group {
>>   	struct list_head active_bg_list;
>>   	struct work_struct zone_finish_work;
>>   	struct extent_buffer *last_eb;
>> -	u64 reclaim_mark;
>>   };
>>   
>>   static inline u64 btrfs_block_group_end(const struct btrfs_block_group *block_group)
>> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/space-info.c b/fs/btrfs/space-info.c
>> index e6641597b321e..39a28e1bec8ad 100644
>> --- a/fs/btrfs/space-info.c
>> +++ b/fs/btrfs/space-info.c
>> @@ -2156,7 +2156,7 @@ static bool do_reclaim_sweep(struct btrfs_space_info *space_info, int raid)
>>   			will_reclaim = true;
>>   			reclaim = true;
>>   		}
>> -		bg->reclaim_mark++;
>> +		bg->reclaim_mark = true;
>>   		spin_unlock(&bg->lock);
>>   		if (reclaim)
>>   			btrfs_mark_bg_to_reclaim(bg);
>> -- 
>> 2.54.0
>>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 0/9] dax/kmem: atomic whole-device hotplug via sysfs

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 0/9] dax/kmem: atomic whole-device hotplug via sysfs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/9] mm/memory: add memory_block_aligned_range() helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function called `memory_block_aligned_range()` to the Linux kernel's memory management code. The function takes an input range and aligns its start address up and end address down to memory block boundaries, which is required for memory hotplug operations. This helper is added as a common function in `<linux/memory.h>` and is used by the `dax/kmem` driver to ensure that memory ranges are properly aligned. The patch also updates the `dax/kmem` code to use this new helper function instead of manually aligning the range. The goal of this patch is to simplify the process of handling memory hotplug operations and reduce the risk of errors due to misaligned ranges.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/9] mm/memory_hotplug: pass online_type to online_memory_block() via arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the `online_memory_block()` function to accept an online type through its argument, rather than using a default value. This change allows callers to specify explicit online types and prepares for future modifications.

review_comments.author: Gupta, Pankaj
review_comments.summary: Pankaj Gupta reviewed the patch and gave it a Reviewed-by tag without providing any specific feedback or concerns.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/9] mm/memory_hotplug: export mhp_get_default_online_type
series_items.patch_summary: This patch exports the `mhp_get_default_online_type` function from the Linux kernel's memory hotplug module, allowing drivers to use it even when hotplug is disabled. The function returns a default online type of `MMOP_OFFLINE`, which is non-destructive and suitable for DAX use cases. This change enables drivers like CXL to build and use DAX without requiring hotplug as a predicate. The patch also adds an inline implementation for the function in case it's not enabled by CONFIG_MEMORY_HOTPLUG.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/9] mm/memory_hotplug: add __add_memory_driver_managed() with online_type arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new interface to the Linux kernel's memory hotplug functionality, allowing drivers to select their preferred online type (ZONE_NORMAL or ZONE_MOVABLE) when adding memory. The CXL driver is the primary motivator for this change, as it needs to plumb a preferred policy through the dax kmem driver. The patch refactors existing APIs and adds new helpers to support this feature, while ensuring that only known in-tree modules can override the system-wide onlining policy.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/9] mm/memory_hotplug: offline_and_remove_memory_ranges()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function called offline_and_remove_memory_ranges() that allows callers to manage multiple contiguous memory ranges as one operation under a single lock. The function takes an array of ranges and processes them in two phases: first, it offlines every block of every range, and then it removes the ranges only if all ranges are offline. If any offline fails, the whole operation is reverted, providing callers with all-or-nothing semantics for the offline step. This resolves a race condition between system daemons trying to manage the same resource by giving them all-or-nothing semantics. The existing offline_and_remove_memory() function becomes a thin wrapper that passes its single range to the new helper.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/9] dax: plumb hotplug online_type through dax
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows drivers that use the dax_kmem driver to specify an auto-online policy when creating a DAX device. It adds a new field, 'online_type', to the DAX device creation path, which can be used to override the system default online policy. The system default is still used if no explicit policy is specified, and this change does not introduce any behavioral changes for existing callers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 7/9] dax/kmem: extract hotplug/hotremove helper functions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the kmem probe and remove functions by extracting helper functions to manage memory hotplug operations. The new helpers, dax_kmem_init_resources, dax_kmem_cleanup_resources, dax_kmem_do_hotplug, and dax_kmem_do_hotremove, enable future extensions for more granular control over memory hotplug. This refactoring is necessary to support hot-add functionality by separating resource management from hotunplug/cleanup operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 8/9] dax/kmem: add sysfs interface for atomic whole-device hotplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a sysfs interface to enable atomic whole-device hotplug operations for DAX kmem devices. It introduces a new 'state' attribute that allows devices to be transitioned between different states, including unplugged, online, and offline. The patch also includes changes to prevent deadlock during unbind operations and to handle legacy auto_online_blocks=offline policy. The atomic whole-device hotplug operation is implemented using the dax_kmem_do_hotremove() function, which collects all added ranges and offlines/removes them in one operation. This patch addresses a race condition that existed when trying to offline and remove an entire multi-block DAX kmem device.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 9/9] selftests/dax: add dax/kmem hotplug sysfs regression test
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a selftest to the Linux kernel's dax subsystem, specifically targeting the kmem hotplug sysfs interface. The test checks that the 'state' attribute of a kmem-backed dax device transitions correctly between unplugged, online, and online_movable states. It also verifies that force-unbinding does not deadlock and that invalid input is rejected. Additionally, it tests a specific regression scenario where re-onlineing after unplug must re-reserve per-range resources to ensure memory removal.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds atomic whole-device hotplug control to the dax kmem driver via a sysfs 'state' attribute, allowing for transitions across entire regions of memory to be performed atomically and safely. The current implementation has a race condition between offlining and removing memory blocks, which this patch aims to solve by introducing a new ABI with state names mirroring per-block memoryX/state attributes. The series also includes mm preparation changes to support the new hotplug control, including adding helpers for online type management and atomic offline+remove of multiple ranges. The dax/kmem feature is updated to plumb online_type through its device creation path and extract hotplug/hotremove into helper functions. A selftest is added to regression test the new attribute.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges that some reviewer comments remain unaddressed and plans to re-spin the patch in version 6, after the merge window closes.
review_comments.raw_body:

to respin this.  Will wait until after the merge window closes for v6.

The rough shape of things should still hold w/ prior feedback.

~Gregory

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/3] nfsd: inter-server copy fixes

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/3] nfsd: fix cpntf publish race in nfs4_init_cp_state
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a race condition in the NFSv4 server (nfsd) that occurs when publishing a copy notify state. The issue arises from the fact that the publication and linkage of the state to the cp_list are split across two separate critical sections, leaving a window where the entry is reachable but not yet linked. A concurrent request can exploit this window, leading to a NULL dereference and server crash. To fix this, the patch folds the cs_type assignment and list_add into the same critical section as idr_alloc_cyclic, initializes cp_list with INIT_LIST_HEAD after allocation, and switches _free_cpntf_state_locked() to use list_del_init().

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/3] nfsd: fix UAF in cpntf statelist drain
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free (UAF) bug in the NFS server's statelist drain function, nfs4_free_cpntf_statelist(). The bug occurs when a concurrent holder increments the reference count of a state while another thread is draining the list. The fix involves rewriting the drain to unconditionally revoke each entry before dropping its parent-owned reference, ensuring that all entries are properly removed from the list and freed in one pass.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/3] nfsd: fix UAF in async copy cancel and shutdown
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free (UAF) bug in the NFS daemon's async copy cancel and shutdown functionality. The issue arises when an async copy is canceled or shut down, causing the reaper to move the copy off the async_copies list while another thread tries to access it, leading to a double free of the filecache object. To fix this, the patch modifies find_async_copy() to clear cp_clp and remove the copy from async_copies after incrementing its reference count, ensuring that the reaper can no longer observe the copy. Additionally, nfsd4_stop_copy() is modified to release the list-membership reference held by the reaper before calling cleanup_async_copy().

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series fixes bugs in the inter-server copy offload code of the NFS daemon, specifically addressing a publish race condition and use-after-free issues in the cpntf state management.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] blkcg: update iocost_coef_gen.py to use io_uring

version_history.subject: [PATCH] blkcg: update iocost_coef_gen.py to use io_uring

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch updates the iocost_coef_gen.py script to use io_uring instead of libaio for benchmarking fast disks, allowing it to generate enough throughput to saturate the disk.

review_comments.author: Tejun Heo
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch without any objections or suggestions.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Jun 24, 2026 at 10:50:34AM -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> Recently I found myself having to benchmark some rather fast disks for
> iocost, but the old iocost_coef_gen.py script couldn't generate enough
> throughput to saturate it. Make it use io_uring instead.
> 
> Cc: Tejun Heo <tj@kernel.org>
> Cc: linux-block@vger.kernel.org
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

Acked-by: Tejun Heo <tj@kernel.org>

Thanks.

-- 
tejun
